common_name: Stonehead Cabbage (AAS-Winning Compact Early Green Cabbage)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9, transplant late August through September for November–January harvest. Stonehead is also excellent for spring production: start indoors January–February and transplant when temperatures allow. The compact size makes Stonehead ideal for small gardens where a full-sized cabbage would take too much space; plant at 12–15 inch spacing for full-sized heads or 10 inch spacing for smaller, tennis-ball-size heads.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 65–75°F; harden off before transplanting to full sun in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil. Stonehead is a 1969 All-America Selections-winning hybrid cabbage that earned recognition for combining early maturity with compact plant size — producing well-formed, 3–5 lb solid round heads on plants that occupy roughly half the space of standard cabbage types. The 'stone' in the name refers to the rock-solid firmness of the tightly packed, dense heads — when thumped, a properly matured Stonehead sounds and feels like a stone ball. The compact habit (spread of 12–15 inches vs. the 18–24 inches of standard types) at 12-inch spacing allows 25–30% more plants per row, making Stonehead one of the most space-efficient cabbage varieties for home gardens. The heads are round, attractively formed, medium blue-green with smooth skin (not savoy-type), and crack-resistant — holding in the garden for an extended period after reaching maturity without splitting. The mild, tender cabbage flavor is excellent raw in coleslaw, fermented as sauerkraut, or cooked; the dense texture holds up well in both applications. Stonehead remains one of the most widely recommended cabbage varieties in the United States more than 55 years after its AAS recognition.
sources: All-America Selections — Stonehead cabbage AAS winner 1969
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cabbage variety comparison
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Cabbage Production Guide

common_name: Red Express Cabbage (Early Small Red Cabbage for Fall Production)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Red Express is particularly well-suited to fall production — the cool temperatures of fall intensify the anthocyanin red-purple color and sweeten the flavor. In zone 9, transplant mid-August through September for October–December harvest. The small, compact heads (1.5–3 lbs) reach maturity in just 63–72 days from transplant and hold in the garden without splitting for several weeks after reaching maturity.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 65–75°F; transplant to full sun in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil. Red Express is an early-maturing red cabbage that matures 2–3 weeks faster than standard red varieties like Red Acre or Ruby Ball, producing small to medium (1.5–3 lb), nicely rounded, deep purple-red heads in just 63–72 days from transplant. Red cabbages in general contain significantly more anthocyanin antioxidants than green types and have a characteristic slightly sharper, more peppery flavor profile; Red Express delivers these attributes in an early-maturing, compact form well-suited to small gardens and fall production windows where there may not be time for the standard 80–85 day maturity of most red cabbage varieties. The red-purple pigmentation intensifies with cool fall temperatures — heads started in late summer and maturing in fall have dramatically deeper, more saturated color than spring-grown heads. Red Express holds well in the garden after reaching maturity and stores very well in refrigeration (4–6 months at 32°F), making it an excellent variety for gardeners who want to put up a supply of red cabbage for winter coleslaw and braised preparations.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Red Express cabbage variety notes
High Mowing Organic Seeds — Red cabbage variety comparison
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Cabbage Production Guide

common_name: Green Goliath Broccoli (Large-Head Side-Shooting Heirloom Broccoli)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Green Goliath produces a large central head followed by prolific side shoots that continue for weeks after the main head is harvested. In zone 9, transplant August–September for November–January harvest. For spring production, start indoors January–February and transplant when temperatures allow — spring-harvested broccoli is excellent in the Gulf South before temperatures rise.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 65–75°F; transplant to full sun, fertile, well-drained soil with good calcium availability. Harvest the central head while florets are still tight and before the head begins to open or yellow; side shoots will continue for 4–6 weeks. Green Goliath is an open-pollinated broccoli that produces large central heads — typically 8–10 inches across and 1–2 lbs — with the classic well-domed, dark green form that represents the traditional ideal of quality broccoli. The 'goliath' name refers to the head size: substantially larger central heads than many modern hybrid varieties that have been bred for smaller, more uniform heads suited to commercial harvest. After the central head is harvested, Green Goliath produces abundant and persistent side shoots — small 2–4 inch heads — that continue for 4–6 weeks, extending the productive season significantly beyond a single harvest. The side-shooting characteristic makes Green Goliath particularly valuable for home gardeners who want an extended harvest rather than a single concentrated yield. The open-pollinated status means seed can be saved from year to year, making it a popular choice for seed savers and those interested in maintaining productive self-sustaining vegetable gardens. Very good flavor at both the head and side-shoot stages.
sources: Burpee Seeds — Green Goliath broccoli variety notes
Seed Savers Exchange — Open-pollinated broccoli collection
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Broccoli Production Guide

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Arcadia is specifically valued for its exceptional cold tolerance — the variety holds its heads in quality through heavy frosts and cool temperatures that would damage most broccoli. In zone 9, transplant August–September for November–January harvest. Arcadia's ability to hold heads without yellowing or loosening during cold weather provides a wider harvest window than cold-sensitive varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 65–75°F; transplant to full sun, fertile, well-drained soil with pH 6.0–7.0. Arcadia is an F1 hybrid broccoli that has become a benchmark variety for fall and overwintering broccoli production in zones 6–8 — valued specifically for the combination of excellent cold tolerance, uniformly dark blue-green head color, and disease resistance. Most broccoli varieties produce their best heads in the narrow temperature range of 60–65°F and deteriorate rapidly when exposed to hard frosts — heads yellow, open up, or develop loose, poor-quality florets. Arcadia was selected specifically for the ability to hold tight, well-formed heads through significantly colder temperatures, allowing gardeners and growers in the mid-Atlantic and Pacific Northwest to harvest marketable broccoli after hard frosts in October and November that would compromise most other varieties. The deep blue-green color of Arcadia heads is exceptionally attractive — darker and more uniform than many broccoli varieties — and holds through the cold exposure. Disease resistance includes downy mildew, black rot, and clubroot, which are important fall disease concerns in humid climates. Arcadia is a top recommendation from Johnny's Selected Seeds for fall production and has a loyal following among market gardeners who rely on fall broccoli as a key late-season crop.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Arcadia broccoli variety notes and trial results
Osborne Seed Company — Arcadia broccoli description
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Fall Broccoli Variety Evaluation

common_name: Jade Pagoda Chinese Cabbage (Tall Columnar Napa Cabbage)

planting.note: Direct sow in well-tilled, fertile soil or start indoors and transplant; Jade Pagoda is best grown as a fall crop in zone 9 — direct sow August through September for harvest November through January. Spring crops can bolt in warming weather; direct sow March for early spring harvest. Thin to 12–15 inches for full-sized heads. Chinese cabbage is a heavy feeder and benefits from consistent moisture and regular nitrogen applications.

directions: Direct sow seeds 0.5 inch deep in well-tilled, fertile soil; thin to 12–15 inches for full-sized heads. Jade Pagoda is a tall, columnar Chinese cabbage (napa cabbage) that differs in form from the more squat, barrel-shaped types like Michihili and standard napa types — the heads are distinctly elongated (14–18 inches tall, 8–10 inches wide), with a compact, upright, tower-like form reminiscent of a pagoda spire, which gives the variety its name. The elongated form produces a higher proportion of the central, tightly blanched, pale yellow-green interior leaves relative to total head weight — the most tender and mildly flavored portion — compared to rounder, more compact types. Jade Pagoda has excellent storage quality: the tight, dense heads keep well in refrigeration (4–6 weeks) without significant quality loss. The flavor is mild, slightly sweet, and delicate — the characteristic Chinese cabbage flavor that is far milder than Western cabbage types — excellent raw in salads (particularly Korean kimchi-style preparations), cooked in stir-fries and soups, and in the braised Chinese preparations where the sweet, soft, cooked texture complements soy and ginger sauces. An excellent variety for gardeners who want to grow Chinese cabbage and experiment with Korean or Asian cooking traditions.
sources: Bejo Seeds — Jade Pagoda Chinese cabbage variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Chinese cabbage variety comparison
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Chinese Cabbage Production
